title: CO-97 Denial Code: Bundled Billing — What It Means and How to Dispute It
meta_description: CO-97 means a service was included in a bundled payment for another procedure. Find out when you can dispute a CO-97 denial and exactly how to do it.
target_problem: Patient received a CO-97 denial and wants to know if the bundling was applied correctly and how to dispute it.
body:
CO-97 is a Claim Adjustment Reason Code that means the benefit for a service is included in the payment for another service that has already been adjudicated. In plain terms: your insurer bundled two services together and denied separate payment for one of them.

CO-97 is one of the most frequent administrative denial codes, particularly for outpatient procedures, lab work performed during surgery, and multi-step treatments billed on the same date.

Most CO-97 denials trace back to the National Correct Coding Initiative (NCCI), a CMS program that identifies pairs of procedure codes that should be billed as one unit rather than two.

A CO-97 denial may be disputable when: the services were performed on different dates; a modifier (-59 or XS) applies to show the services were distinct; or the bundling rule does not apply to your specific plan.

To dispute: request your EOB, check whether a modifier applies, verify the dates of service, and file a formal appeal if needed with documentation that the services were distinct.

This content does not constitute legal or medical advice. MyAdvocate is not a law firm. For questions specific to your situation, consult a qualified attorney or healthcare professional.
